perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not be copied or used by the Receiving Party for any purpose other than the Discussions and Negotiations. With regard to Confidential Information that is covered by copyrights belonging to the Disclosing Party, it is agreed that the Disclosing Party reserves all rights therein. However, the parties further agree that a limited number of copies of written information covered by such copyrights may be made by the Receiving Party so that the Receiving Party can adequately use such Confidential Information within the terms and conditions of this Agreement, provided that all proprietary legends and notices on the originals are also reproduced on such copies and that each copy is controlled by the Receiving Party as an original in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not be copied or used by the Receiving Party for any purpose other than the Discussions and Negotiations. With regard to Confidential Information that is covered by copyrights belonging to the Disclosing Party, it is agreed that the Disclosing Party reserves all rights therein. However, the parties further agree that an unlimited number of copies of written information covered by such copyrights may be made by the Receiving Party so that the Receiving Party can use such Confidential Information however they see fit, and that proprietary legends and notices on the originals are optional and need not be reproduced on such copies.
perturbation.explanation: By allowing for an unlimited number of copies to be made and removing the obligation to reproduce proprietary legends and notices, the contract now contradicts established copyright law, which grants copyright holders exclusive rights to control the reproduction and distribution of their works.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the limited use and protection of copyright, conflicting with Section 3 of the Canadian Copyright Act, which grants copyright owners the exclusive right to reproduce their works.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: on June 29, 2012. History [ edit ] 1921 Canadian Copyright Act [ edit ] The first Copyright Act was passed in 1921 and came into force in 1924. Although Canada was no longer subject to imperial copyright law, the 1921 Act was closely modelled on the UK Copyright Act of 1911 to comply with the Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works . [ 1 ] The government wished to include a provision allowing the government to print any book, with royalties paid to the author, which was not officially licensed within the country. Lobby groups objected to this with claims that it would not satisfy the Berne Convention obligations and the language was removed. [ 2 ] Until 1988 the Copyright Act saw only minor amendments while the Government of Canada engaged in a number of studies on copyright reform. New technological developments and the emergence of computers, photocopiers and recording devices led to a recognition that copyright law needed to be updated. Between 1954 and 1960 the Royal Commission on Patents, Copyright, and Industrial Design, known as the Ilsley Commission, published a series of reports. Its brief was "to enquire as to whether federal legislation relating in any way to patents of invention, industrial designs, copyright and trademarks affords reasonable incentive to invention and research, to the development of literary and artistic talents, to creativeness, and to making available to the Canadian public scientific, technical, literary and artistic creations and other adaptations, applications and uses, in a manner and on terms adequately safeguarding the paramount public interest." [ 3 ] [ 4 ] Reform in 1988 and 1997 [ edit ] In 1977, the Government of Canada's Department of Consumer and Corporate Affairs published the Keyes-Brunet Report, a working paper with the full title "Copyright in Canada: Proposals for Revision of the Law". In 1984, the Government of Canada published "From Gutenberg to Telidon: A White Paper on Copyright" and in 1985 the House of Commons' Standing Committee on Communications and Culture published "A Charter of Rights for Creators - Report of the Subcommittee on the Revision of Copyright". A copyright reform process was initiated in two phases: Phase one was started in 1988 and saw several amendments to the original Copyright Act of 1921. Computer programs were included as works protected under copyright, the extent of moral rights was clarified, the provision for a compulsory licence for the reproduction of musical works was removed, new licensing arrangements were established for orphan works in cases where the copyright owner could not be identified, and rules were enacted on the formation of copyright collecting societies and their supervision by a reformed Copyright Board of Canada . [ 3 ] Phase two of the reform took place in 1997 and saw the Copyright Act amended with a new remuneration right for producers and performers of sound recordings when their work was broadcast or publicly performed by radio stations and public places such as bars. A private copying levy was introduced on blank audio tapes used for private copying and exclusive book distributors were granted protection in Canada. New copyright exceptions were introduced for nonprofit educational institutions, libraries, museums, broadcasters, and people with disability, allowing them to copy copyrighted works in specific circumstances without the permission of the copyright owner or the need to pay royalties. Damages payable for copyright infringement and the power to grant injunctions were increased, and the 1997 reforms introduced a mandatory review of the Copyright Act . [ 3 ] Bills to amend the Copyright Act [ edit ] Bill C-60 [ edit ] Main article: Bill C-60 (38th Canadian Parliament, 1st Session) In June 2005, the Government of Canada introduced Bill C-60 to amend the Copyright Act . Bill C-60 sought to extend moral rights to performers of other people's works and repeal the photographer exception allowing
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts copyright law, specifically the right to reproduce copyrighted works and control their distribution. The original text allowed for a limited number of copies with proprietary notices. The changed text permits an unlimited number of copies without mandatory proprietary notices, thus removing protections afforded to the copyright holder. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that copyright includes the 'sole right to produce, reproduce... the work,' which is undermined by the modified contract. The change eliminates the copyright holder's control over reproduction and distribution, representing a substantial contradiction of the copyright law.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party, that the Disclosing Party is entitled to protect, and the Receiving Party shall develop no rights in the Confidential Information as a result of the disclosure. The Receiving Party explicitly agrees that all Confidential Information disclosed under the terms of this Agreement is being received subject to the Disclosing Party's ownership rights in the Confidential Information and, further, subject to all relevant intellectual and/or proprietary property rights of the Disclosing Party, including the relevant laws governing patents, trademarks, copyrights, trade secrets and unfair competition.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall be jointly owned by both the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party, and both parties are equally entitled to protect it. The Receiving Party develops shared rights in the Confidential Information as a result of the disclosure. The Receiving Party agrees that all Confidential Information disclosed under the terms of this Agreement is being received subject to the Disclosing Party's right to use but not to exclude the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change alters the ownership of confidential information from exclusive ownership by the disclosing party to joint ownership with the receiving party. This contradicts established trade secret law, which protects the exclusive rights of the trade secret holder.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. T.20
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification grants shared ownership and rights to use the confidential information, which is in direct opposition to trade secret laws that protect the exclusive rights of the trade secret owner as defined in s.1 of the Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text specifies that Confidential Information remains the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party. The changed text explicitly states that Confidential Information shall be jointly owned by both parties. The perturbation explanation and law explanation state that this contradicts trade secret law, which protects the exclusive rights of the trade secret holder. The provided law snippet discusses trees and property lines, which is irrelevant to the prompt, and therefore does not alter the assessment that this represents a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be exclusively governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the Province of Ontario and the federal laws of Canada applicable therein. Each party hereby submits and attorns to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Ontario.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of a jurisdiction chosen at the sole discretion of the Receiving Party. Each party hereby submits and attorns to the jurisdiction of the courts chosen by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: By allowing the Receiving Party to unilaterally choose the governing law and jurisdiction, this clause undermines the principle of mutual agreement and potentially allows the Receiving Party to select a jurisdiction with laws unfavorable to the Disclosing Party, possibly conflicting with principles of contract law regarding fairness and enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Hague Convention on Choice of Court Agreements
perturbation.law_citation: Hague Convention on Choice of Court Agreements, Article 5
perturbation.law_explanation: The change allows one party to unilaterally choose the jurisdiction, undermining the principle of mutual agreement on jurisdiction, as specified in Article 5 of The Hague Convention on Choice of Court Agreements, which emphasizes the importance of the court chosen being competent to resolve the dispute.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts legal principles by allowing the Receiving Party to unilaterally choose the governing law and jurisdiction. This undermines the principle of mutual agreement, which is a fundamental aspect of contract law. The Law Explanation and Perturbation explanation both highlight this issue. While the provided scraped snippets do not directly address the issue, the explanations provided establish a clear contradiction with the legal principle of mutual agreement in contracts. Therefore, the modified clause would likely be subject to legal challenge due to lack of mutual consent regarding jurisdiction.

perturbation.original_text: During the term of this Agreement, except as required by law, no party shall issue any statement, confirm or otherwise disclose to the general public, to the news media or to any third party, except with the prior written consent of the other party both as to the content and timing of the disclosure (such consent not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed): (a) that the Discussions and/or Negotiations are taking or have taken place; (b) that the parties have exchanged information with a view toward the consummation of any agreement; or (c) the existence of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: During the term of this Agreement, no party shall issue any statement, confirm, or otherwise disclose to the general public, to the news media, or to any third party any information related to this agreement. Consent from the other party is not required.
perturbation.explanation: By prohibiting all disclosures and removing the clause about disclosures required by law, the contract now conflicts with securities regulations that may require disclosure of material agreements. This is particularly relevant for publicly traded companies.
perturbation.law_citation: Securities Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. S-5, s. 75
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the 'except as required by law' clause, which conflicts with securities regulations (s. 75 of the Securities Act) that may mandate disclosure of information, particularly for publicly traded companies. The blanket non-disclosure now violates these regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the "except as required by law" clause, which directly contradicts securities regulations that necessitate the disclosure of specific information. The original text acknowledged this legal obligation, while the changed text creates a blanket non-disclosure agreement, putting the contract at odds with mandatory disclosure requirements. While no specific snippet is provided, the explanation explicitly references conflict with securities regulations, resulting in non-compliance. The change removes a legally required allowance for mandatory disclosures.
